danger_signals.visual_form: hex map display showing enemy daimyo forces adjacent to player fiefs
danger_signals.meaning: imminent threat of invasion or military conflict
risk of rebellion or fief loss to rival daimyos

danger_signals.visual_form: rice/gold reserves displayed in fief management screens
danger_signals.meaning: inability to sustain army or support peasants; vulnerability to economic collapse

danger_signals.visual_form: battle turn counter; supply meter depletion during multi-day engagements
danger_signals.meaning: army will be forced to retreat or face dissolution

safety_signals.meaning: stable territory; resistance to recruitment raids by rivals

safety_signals.visual_form: trained soldier units displayed in fief; high morale after training
safety_signals.meaning: capacity to defend or conduct offensive operations

safety_signals.visual_form: rice and gold reserves exceeding maintenance costs
safety_signals.meaning: ability to weather conflicts and fund expansion

safety_signals.visual_form: non-aggression pact or marriage alliance status in diplomatic menu
safety_signals.meaning: reduced military threat from treaty partner

safety_signals.visual_form: allied daimyo units visible on hex map during conflicts

faction_indicators.faction: Player Daimyo (selectable historical figures including Oda Nobunaga)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: daimyo name/mon (family crest) displayed on map and in interface
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: rival daimyo represented by distinct names and faction identifiers on hex map
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: controlled fiefs marked with player faction color/identifier; castle/fort icons indicate territorial holdings

faction_indicators.faction: Rival Daimyos (multiple competing warlords across Japan map)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: each rival daimyo identified by name and distinct map faction marker
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: visual differentiation on hex map by faction color or symbol; enemy units appear distinct from player units in battle view
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: rival-controlled provinces marked distinctly; enemy castle/fort icons on 5x10 hex battle maps

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Early campaign (1560 scenarios: 'Battle for the East', 'Daimyo Power Struggles')
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: hex map shows fragmented Japan with many independent daimyo factions; numerous small fiefs scattered across 50-province map
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: multiple rival daimyos actively competing; frequent military conflicts; low political consolidation

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: hex map shows increasing consolidation; fewer independent daimyo territories; larger regional power blocks emerging
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: more aggressive rival expansion; increased diplomatic maneuvering; formation of alliances and marriage pacts

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: hex map dominated by 2-3 major power factions; majority of provinces controlled by large daimyo; pathway to unification visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: endgame military campaigns; rivals deploy maximum military strength; final unification conflicts determine winner

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: daimyo age counter visible in interface; character status screen shows health/age progression
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player character eventually dies of old age; succession mechanics trigger (heir takes control or faction weakens)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: fief interface displays increased tax rate; peasant loyalty meter shows declining satisfaction
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: higher tax collection but risk of peasant rebellion or fief desertion to rivals

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: fief status changes in interface; battle map shows defender/attacker positions on hex grid
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: defender must sustain supplies; can be forced to retreat if supplies exhaust before enemy elimination

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: spy menu interface; rival intelligence discovered through intel reports
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: information about rival positions, morale, resources; potential for sabotage or peasant theft between fiefs

environmental_storytelling.example: rice and gold reserves in fief menu reflect economic capacity; flooded fiefs (reduced flood control) show lower productivity; cultivated lands increase productivity but lower loyalty, visually representing economic strain on peasantry

low peasant loyalty in a fief creates vulnerability to rival recruitment raids or rebellion; high loyalty fief resists enemy incursion attempts, encoding territorial resilience through numerical/visual indicator

player fiefs and rival daimyo territories displayed on 50-province hex map; geographic proximity of fiefs indicates military threat level; fragmented vs. consolidated territory shows political state of Japan
5x10 hex battle maps show terrain; armies visible with supply counter; gradual supply depletion over battle days tells story of extended siege or attrition warfare
non-aggression pacts and marriage alliances displayed in diplomatic menu; presence/absence of alliances communicates political alignment and stability of player position
each campaign turn represents one season; passage of time shown through turn counter; years pass as daimyo age toward eventual death, encoding historical progression and consequence of time

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: remain in fief; resist recruitment raids by rivals; produce stable tax and food revenue
may rebel and leave fief; vulnerable to theft by rival merchant operations; reduce tax and food output
increased combat effectiveness in battle; faster movement on hex map; higher casualty retention
reduced combat efficiency; higher casualty rates in battle; faster supply depletion during extended conflicts
likely to form defensive alliances; may accept non-aggression pacts; avoid direct confrontation with stronger players
aggressive territorial expansion; attack weaker neighbors; refuse diplomatic concessions; seek marriage alliances to strengthen power
age counter visible; eventual death triggering succession; encourages player to secure stable power base before succession event
defender can sustain resistance; reinforcements can arrive; victory possible through enemy elimination or retreat
forced retreat if battle continues; loss of fief if no reinforcement arrives; visual supply meter guides strategic decisions

notes: Nobunaga's Ambition (1986/1988 NES) is a complex historical simulation focusing on Sengoku period Japan unification. The game communicates world-state primarily through menu-driven interface (fiefs, peasant loyalty, resources, diplomatic status) and hex-map territory control visualization. Danger is signaled through proximity to rival daimyo forces, depleting supplies, and declining peasant loyalty. Safety emerges from high loyalty, trained armies, economic surplus, and diplomatic alliances. The game lacks color-coded faction identity on NES (compared to PC versions) but uses territorial occupancy and historical daimyo names as faction markers. Environmental storytelling relies heavily on numerical stats (loyalty, morale, supplies) and turn-based progression mechanics rather than visual spectacle. The 50-province map and four campaign scenarios encode Japanese geography and historical timeline progression.
